speaker: interviewer
text: It's December 7, 1995. Gerda, we left off on the last tape with you telling the story about an essay that you had written and, and that your father wanted to talk to you about it. Could you continue the story, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. And I think it was totally accepted that you, you know, you were not too conspicuous. You don't draw too much attention to yourself. I mean, I didn't find this particularly painful. That's the way things were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, as, as a Jew, there were some things that you couldn't do, that you shouldn't have done. But by and large, I think I felt rather secure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changes did you observe as Hitler rose to power in Germany in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- and I'm not quite sure what this particular date was-- but I remember one time seeing my father cry. It was the first time I saw my father cry. And I was told they were-- they were burning books in Germany. Later on tales, of course, filtered in what was happening. I will say my parents usually dismissed it, and they said it couldn't be. It was the country of Goethe, Franz Ferdinand, so many-- it couldn't happen in Germany. I mean, those things must be exaggerated.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it rang closer when, when he marched into, into Austria because we knew people and had some family in, in Austria and they came. But, you know, children were shielded from that. Don't forget when Hitler came to power in 1933, I was nine years old. And a European child of nine-- I mean even, even at age 15, if I compare it with my grandchildren I think I was tremendously-- it was very unsophisticated, you know? And you didn't get the news and certain things were not told in front of the children. So I would say that I really did not know terribly much about it. So your life wasn't in any great way affected from 1933--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened when the Germans invaded your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My God, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, perhaps I should go a little bit before that. My, my maternal grandmother died in, in April of 1938. My-- my-- I remember my uncle from Turkey had come then and there was a bit of talk-- quite a bit of talk-- about it of what was going on in Germany. I think this is really when I learned a little more. But my mother and I-- my mother was not terribly well. And we were away in the summer, I would say probably from June till late, August middle August-- I'm not quite sure-- of '39. We were in a summer place called Krinitze.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would say the first impact came on, on a day when we attended a concert. And a young man, open neck shirt-- which, you know, it was very informal for, for this particular setting-- pushed the conductor aside and addressed the crowd. And he says, how can you sit here all dressed up listening to music?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he pointed to the mountaintops which separated us from Czechoslovakia. And Czechoslovakia was already occupied. And he said disaster is coming from there. Go home. Pick up knives. Pick up weapons. And fight the enemy. And I remember moving-- and pretty soon the police came. And he was arrested for disturbing the peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember moving closer to my mother and being very frightened and asking her if we should go home. And people started talking, said-- everybody said, you know, that's ridiculous. What is he talking about? But a couple days later, I think we had-- or maybe my mother sent my father a telegram. I don't remember. But I do remember that we went home earlier than expected. Perhaps we were supposed to stay there until the end of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember then as we passed different train station, we saw a lot of luggage which was all marked for Warszawa, which means Warsaw. People were sending belongings away. When we came home, my father didn't look well. He had his arm in a sling. And he said he had some trouble with his arm. My mother was very concerned. And we found out my father had a slight heart attack, and he was put into bed. And from then on, of course, everything centered around my father. People started to come. My mother didn't want to turn the radio on. My father shouldn't hear the news. And people were cautioned not to mention much in front of my father. He needed peace, and he needed rest. People were saying that they were going to leave. It looked very dangerous. Shortly thereafter, a telegram came from my uncle-- my mother's brother in Turkey-- telling us that we should get out of Poland that we have visas at the Warsaw embassy. My mother put the telegram in her pocket and she said, Papa is ill. That's our first concern. And that was the verdict of death for our family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On Friday morning, the 1st of September 1939, was an enormous row of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] planes. Everybody went into the streets, and we saw lots of planes with swastikas were all flying over. You know, we were just a few miles from the front here. And there's some hysteria. Everybody started going crazy. They were digging all kinds of trenches, and it was a horrible night. I remember I was sitting in my parents' bedroom with my, my parents and my brother. And, and there were explosions all over the place. And we saw people running away. Streets were full of people with horses, with buggies, with side cars, with animals, with children. It was just a frightening sort of thing. And my father said to me, go downstairs and call the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went down, and I started calling uncles and aunts. There were no answer. When I came up, my father said, nobody is there is there? And I said, no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And apparently everybody had left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day was Saturday. And I remember that is the most wonderful day. It was a very peaceful day. There was no telephone. The radio didn't work. Electricity didn't work. My father was out of bed suddenly, which seemed great. And but at night-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] at night there was shooting. My brother went outside to let my cat in. And he said there were people on roofs. They were shooting. And they were saying that the Germans were coming. And we went to the basement. And I'm a little fuzzy then but the next thing that I remember very clearly. We're sitting in the dining room and people going in sort of a Sunday morning. And people were all dressed up carrying flowers and cameras. And my brother or maybe my mother said, you don't think that they-- and at this point the first Nazi motorcycle road down the street. We saw the uniforms, the swastika. And my brother, he had his wristwatch in his pocket, and he pulled it out. And it fell to the floor. It was 9:10 in the morning, September 3, 1939. And then the nightmare began.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was unbelievable, you know? Suddenly flags with swastikas sprouted from the rooftops across the street. People were carrying cakes and drinks and yelling, you know, heil Hitler. Long live the fuhrer. And thank you for liberation. And suddenly you found yourself being the-- a stranger being at home. And we didn't realize. I mean, how come everybody was prepared, and, and we didn't know anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first act was to burn the temple with some-- with people in it. Then the horror stories started coming. You know, a neighbor came and warned us that my father and my brother should hide. That people were taken off the street and off their homes and all atrocities. You know, that was Sunday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, we were so close to the border because the war allegedly was declared. Of course, there was no declaration of war. They just went across the border on Friday morning. And we were already occupied on Sunday morning at 9:10. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it was all over. The life we knew, the life we were a part of, it was all gone. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything changed. Suddenly it was fear. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My, my father-- my mother, you know, was almost obsessed that nobody should see my father. My father was still ill and particularly [INAUDIBLE] my brother. And suddenly my mother sort of became the authority figure at home, you know? It was my mother who could go out. And, and I was sent on errands, you know? And the worst thing was that the entire family-- which was an enormous family-- nobody was there. Everybody had left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that, that everyone had left and you didn't? What was-- why did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, they had talked to my father. My father knew some of it, but my father couldn't leave. My father was ill. And, you know, people thought that by going into the interior of Poland-- I mean, they knew that when the invasion was coming, it was obviously that it will-- that we would be immediately occupied. But they thought there would be a strong stand, as in fact they thought, you know, Krakow or Warsaw would. But Poland was overrun in 18 days. I mean some people managed to, to escape then what became Russia. But everyone from my family was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other restrictions were placed upon you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost everything. I don't remember how long it took, but it seems to me perhaps just weeks before we had to, you know wear-- at first it was sort of a wide arm band with a blue star. Later on it was a yellow star. And the registration cards were immediately issued, and which, you know, it was stamped Jew. Ration cards, which became, you know, very restrictive. That first fall was, was still not too bad because it-- it was a rich farm region. You could still buy some food. And we still had a little money. But pretty soon that was gone, and my father didn't work. And we didn't have anything. So we had to continuously sell things of what we had. And then later on my mother always did wonderful handiwork. So she let work-- word go by that we would be knitting sweaters. And so we would knit sweaters for people. And Christmas time, around that time, we were thrown out of our home. We had to live in, in the basement. The basement was very cold and very clammy. We didn't have any electric light. We didn't have any running water. Pretty soon a sign appears that we cannot go to our garden. And so we had to live in the basement. And, and my mother and I would knit. And my father would, would read to us. And my brother was taken away almost immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was taken away on the 19th of October. See, the war broke out on the 3rd of September, and my brother and all young men who could be potential leadership or something were taken away right away. My brother was 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when he left, the night that he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah my-- I-- the last evening before he left, I went to his room. And I must have fallen asleep there. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the morning when he left, he, he told me to, to be strong and to take care of our parents. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mo-- my father broke down completely. And my mother, who, you know, used to go into practically a nervous breakdown if, if a teacup didn't sit right suddenly became a force of inc-- of incredible bravery. She did not cry when my brother left. But, but right after that, she ran to the cemetery, and I ran after her. It was a pretty bad scene. I really realized what-- I think I really came then face to face of, of what was-- what was happening to the family. You know, I think with my brother being at home, I, I was shielded from a lot of things by my brother and my father and my mother. You know, I was always sort of the little one, as you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but suddenly I came face to face with the whole horror of this particular situation, my father's illness and his-- you know. He couldn't work. He couldn't support the family, you know? When footsteps approached he was-- my mother insisted that you go-- he goes into the wardrobe so that they didn't see him, after-- particularly after Arthur left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after he left, my mother didn't make his bed for quite a few days to keep the imprint of his head on the pillow. And I think the truth was driven home to me very, very forcefully. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He left in, in October, and I think in November came the first-- we didn't know where he went and there were all kinds of, of rumors, you know. He said he was going-- that they were going to the interior of Poland to build up what was destroyed by the war. But actually they were taken to the interior of Poland to the bank of the river San, which separated us from the Russian occupied part. And they pursued by bullets. Those who were good swimmers swam across. He was an excellent swimmer. And so late in the year, which was very cold, he swam. Oh, I should have mentioned that my, my aunt, my father's sister, did come back with her son and daughter and my, my cousin David. And Arthur and David were together on the transport. My aunt then left with her daughter to go to the place where they had lived before. So she didn't stay with us too long. But my br-- Arthur and David went together. And after that we got the first notice that we would have to leave our homes with 20 pounds of our belongings. And my mother was very ill. She must have suffered a total nervous breakdown because she was a bit incoherent. She was calling for my brother. And my father told me to, to go and to sell everything. People were coming and buying things. That was still before we had to go to the basement, so it must have been in November. And there were some very cruel things that happened. I remember one. My, my parents' 20th wedding anniversary was on the 1st of April. They were married on the 1st of April, which was sort of a family joke always. And they met in 1919, so their wedding anniversary was just before. It was in April of 1939. And my brother and I saved up and we bought a delicate, pink liquor set. And I remember somebody coming to the house and picking up the set. And he took a glass and he dropped it, broke it. And he said, I can't give you much for it because a glass is missing. And, you know, and pretty soon everything was being emptied from the house, some things which were treasures. And I was left with, with some filthy German marks in my hand, which I gave to my father. And we were supposed to, to pack some belongings and to leave. And I remember it was then I heard that a family committed suicide together. And I almost wished that my parents suggested that we should all go to sleep. And I remember I stood at the window, and I was looking outside. And behind me the house was being emptied of all the things I had known all my life. And my father came up behind me, and he said, what you are thinking is very wrong. He said, it's cowardly. It's terrible. And he said, promise me that you're never going to do that. And I didn't answer him. And he turned me around to him, and he said, I want your promise right now. And I said, I promise you. And as a matter of fact, at one point in my life when I came very close to jumping into, into a train after working all day at [NON-ENGLISH] and at some horrible works. And the nights on coal trains, my resistance was really going down. And I said, you know, it's the time. I'll just jump. And it will be a few seconds, and it's going to be all over. And at this precise moment, I felt a pain in my neck [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that the promise came back. It was and my father always knew what I was thinking. And he made me promise not to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So shortly thereafter, they rescinded the order, and we were permitted to stay again. That was sort of going on every six, every seven or eight months. Then they would come and say, you know, we got an order that we have to leave. And then it would be said that if half a kilo of gold could be produced, we would not have to leave. So you know, everybody gave what they had-- a wedding rings, this and that. And people brought in order to stay. And this, of course, was the way in which they extracted everything that one had. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that was going on for a while. My mother got better, as I mentioned, until we moved to the basement. And we were doing knitting, and we got 30 marks for knitting a sweater, which took us almost a week. And at the black market, you could buy a loaf of bread for about that amount of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But people knew us and particularly my nana. And, and she used to bring food and leave it by the basement window. She sort of threw things down, you know, because it was-- the farmers were close by. And there were potatoes and there were turnips and things. So we were really not hungry. I mean, we didn't have plentiful food, and we didn't have any meat or much dairy, but, but we had some food. As long as we were still at home, as bad as it was, it was manageable. We didn't hear from Arthur from a long time, for many months. And then we did get a  letter that he was in Russia. And of course, my parents absolutely rejoiced that at least, you know, he was away. And, and he used to write to me and he told me that he-- that he knew that I was doing what I promised him to do. And that was of course the greatest accolades that I could get from my brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so it went until April of 1942 when we received notice that we had to leave our home to move to a shabby remote quarter of our town which became the Jewish ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name, the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in Biala. It was not in Bielitz. It was in the-- in the other part of-- which was the-- you see, Bielitz became-- because it had been-- they called it a German cultural thing. It used to be in Germany and mostly German speaking. It became immediately part of the German Reich where Biala-- this was the division-- became the [GERMAN]. And you know, we were just a few miles from Auschwitz. It was very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not a closed ghetto. You know, there were only few-- I don't know, maybe there were a couple hundred people. There were mostly old people, almost no men-- certainly no, not any young men. Old men, women, and children. There were several buildings, dilapidated old buildings with sort of a, you know. I don't know what it was before, perhaps some factories things and like tiny apartments. And we had one room, my parents and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to take some of our furniture which, you know, we didn't have much anymore, I mean nothing decent. Just my, my parents' bedroom, that was all and sort of thing on which I slept, you know? And that was loaded on, on a horse drawn thing. You know, there were no trucks, nothing. And my mother and I had to go to work on the ghetto. We were marched-- mostly young girls and women-- to we worked in a shop in Wadowice, which was-- I don't know-- perhaps 40 minutes by train. We were close. The ghetto was close to the train station. And, and then we worked. We got the pittance in, in wages, but, you know, there wasn't much to eat anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on sewing machines, sewing the uniform stuff, stuff like that. And my father had to go to Sucha, which was close by, for the fortification of the river. And since he had a very bad heart condition, it was really very tough on him. You know, it was my mother's horror every day if my father's going to come back. That didn't last too long. We got to the ghetto in April and I turned 18 in May. And, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and I came home one day from work. It was in June. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think it was June 26. I believe it was a Friday. We sort of overheard something that they said that Bielitz is going to be next. When I came home my parents-- there was some agitation. I didn't quite know what was going on. But I was told soon thereafter that we are going to have to leave. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was Saturday night, the 27th of, of June 1942. My father was supposed to leave the next day, Sunday. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I heard my parents talk all that night. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They talked about their lives, their parents, their children, their hopes, their love. They never mentioned what the morning would bring. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think they gave me the most priceless gift that night. They faced the morning with the only weapon they had, their love. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS] My father had to leave the next afternoon. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I have a friend who lives in New York. Her name is-- name used to be Gretel Teichner. She was in camp with me throughout. Her married name is Marchfeld. She reminded me of something that I came to ask her to go with me through the ghetto, and we got hold of all the prayer books. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we took them to the cemetery, which was adjacent to the ghetto because I sort of wanted to do it in honor of my father, that they should not fall into-- I don't know what the Nazi's finally did with it, but I had a pretty good idea of what they might do with it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anyway, I saw my father on the train. He was standing on the platform. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I never saw him again. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The following morning, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] 29th of June, we were woken early. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother-- it was Monday. My mother fasted every Monday because my brother was taken away on Monday, and she somehow thought if she fasted, he will come home. And I begged her that morning to eat something, but she wouldn't. And she made cocoa, which she had hoarded from before the war, and some jam. She made it for me. And so I had really wanted it all throughout the war. I did not enjoy it that morning. I think the scenes of the separations must be well documented from many eyewitnesses. I think I would like to skip it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I will just say that much that we were assembled. I walked with my mother. And there stood [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an SS man with a little piece of wood. He asked me how old I was. I told him 18. He told me to go that way and my mother the other way.
